title: In Praise of Forgetting
summary: An argumentative essay challenging the assumption that perfect digital recall is a gift, and proposing that the capacity to forget is not a flaw of memory but one of its most underrated functions.
body:
We have built, almost without meaning to, a civilisation that no longer forgets. Every message you have sent for a decade sits retrievable on a server somewhere. Your photographs are timestamped, geotagged, and backed up in triplicate. The half-formed opinion you posted at twenty-two waits patiently to be quoted back to you at forty. The prevailing assumption — rarely examined, almost never defended — is that this is straightforwardly an improvement. More memory must be better than less. Who, after all, would choose to forget?

I want to argue the unfashionable position: that forgetting is not a defect we have heroically overcome, but a feature we are recklessly dismantling. The human capacity to let things go is not a bug in the cognitive system. It is part of what allows a life to be lived forward rather than endlessly relitigated.

Consider first what forgetting does for forgiveness. To forgive someone is, in practice, to agree to stop weighting a past offence as heavily as you once did — to let it recede, soften, lose its sharp edges. This is hard enough when memory cooperates by gently blurring the details. It becomes nearly impossible when the original injury is preserved in high definition, available for instant replay, its every word time-stamped and unalterable. A marriage in which both parties can produce the exact transcript of every quarrel is not a marriage with a superior memory. It is a marriage with a prosecutor permanently in residence. The wound never gets the chance to scar over, because the evidence file is always open on the desk, and a grievance that can be reread word for word is a grievance that never quite finishes happening.

The same logic scales upward. A society that cannot forget cannot easily allow people to change. The teenager who said something stupid, the activist whose views matured, the convict who served a sentence and was released — each of these depends, for any prospect of a fresh start, on a degree of public amnesia. We used to call this a second chance. It rested, quietly, on the fact that the record was incomplete and the memory fallible. Strip away that fallibility and you do not get a fairer society; you get one in which every person is permanently chained to the worst version of themselves that was ever captured.

The defenders of total recall will object, not unreasonably, that forgetting also conceals genuine wrongdoing — that the powerful have always relied on the public's short memory to escape accountability. This is true, and I do not wish to wave it away. There is a real and painful tension here, and anyone who pretends otherwise is selling something. The point is not that we should remember nothing. It is that the decision about what to keep and what to release was once made, however imperfectly, by the gentle attrition of time and human limitation. We have now handed that decision to systems that default to keeping everything, and we have done so without ever quite deciding that this was what we wanted.

What troubles me most is the quiet inversion of the burden of proof. Forgetting now requires active effort, justification, even apology — you must request deletion, prove a right to be forgotten, argue your case to a platform. Remembering, by contrast, is the frictionless default. We have arranged our tools so that the easiest thing, the thing that happens when no one does anything at all, is total preservation. And a default, repeated across a billion small moments, is not a neutral technical choice. It is a theory of what a human life is for — and it is a theory I think we would reject, if anyone had thought to ask us.

ý kiến nửa vời, chưa định hình

annotations.meaningVi: một khiếm khuyết mà ta đã anh dũng vượt qua (giọng mỉa mai)

annotations.phrase: a bug in the cognitive system

độ phân giải cao (ở đây dùng ẩn dụ)

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence for emphasis ('It is ... that')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ (cleft sentence) tách một thành phần ra để nhấn mạnh, thường dùng 'It is/was ... that/who'. Trong bài: 'It is part of what allows a life to be lived forward...' và 'The point is not that we should remember nothing. It is that the decision...' Cấu trúc này cho phép tác giả nhấn mạnh chính xác phần thông tin quan trọng, đồng thời tạo nhịp điệu lập luận trang trọng đặc trưng của văn nghị luận C1.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A cleft sentence splits one clause into two to spotlight a particular element. The essay uses the negative-then-positive cleft pair: 'The point is not that we should remember nothing. It is that the decision ... was once made ... by the gentle attrition of time.' This 'not X; it is Y' structure lets the writer correct a likely misreading and emphasise the real claim, a hallmark of careful argument.

translationVi:
Chúng ta đã xây dựng, gần như vô tình, một nền văn minh không còn biết quên. Mọi tin nhắn bạn gửi trong suốt một thập kỷ vẫn nằm đó, có thể truy xuất được trên một máy chủ nào đó. Ảnh của bạn được đóng dấu thời gian, gắn thẻ vị trí, và sao lưu thành ba bản. Cái ý kiến nửa vời bạn đăng năm hai mươi hai tuổi kiên nhẫn chờ đợi để được trích dẫn lại với bạn năm bốn mươi tuổi. Giả định thịnh hành — hiếm khi được xem xét, gần như không bao giờ được bảo vệ — là điều này hiển nhiên là một sự cải tiến. Nhiều trí nhớ hơn hẳn phải tốt hơn ít. Suy cho cùng, ai lại chọn quên?

Tôi muốn lập luận cho lập trường không hợp thời: rằng quên không phải là một khiếm khuyết mà ta đã anh dũng khắc phục, mà là một tính năng ta đang tháo dỡ một cách liều lĩnh. Khả năng buông bỏ của con người không phải là lỗi trong hệ thống nhận thức. Nó là một phần của những gì cho phép một cuộc đời được sống về phía trước thay vì bị đem ra tranh cãi lại mãi mãi.

Hãy xét trước tiên việc quên làm gì cho sự tha thứ. Tha thứ cho ai đó, trong thực tế, là đồng ý ngừng đặt nặng một lỗi lầm trong quá khứ như bạn từng làm — để nó lùi xa, dịu lại, mất đi những góc cạnh sắc nhọn. Điều này đã đủ khó khi trí nhớ chịu hợp tác bằng cách làm mờ nhẹ các chi tiết. Nó trở nên gần như bất khả thi khi vết thương ban đầu được lưu giữ ở độ phân giải cao, sẵn sàng để phát lại tức thì, từng lời được đóng dấu thời gian và không thể thay đổi. Một cuộc hôn nhân mà cả hai bên đều có thể trưng ra bản ghi chính xác của mọi cuộc cãi vã không phải là một cuộc hôn nhân có trí nhớ siêu việt. Nó là một cuộc hôn nhân có một công tố viên thường trú vĩnh viễn. Vết thương không bao giờ có cơ hội kéo da non, vì hồ sơ chứng cứ luôn mở sẵn trên bàn, và một nỗi oán giận có thể đọc lại từng chữ là một nỗi oán giận không bao giờ thật sự kết thúc.

Logic ấy mở rộng lên quy mô lớn hơn. Một xã hội không thể quên thì không dễ cho phép con người thay đổi. Cậu thiếu niên từng nói điều gì đó ngu ngốc, nhà hoạt động có quan điểm đã chín chắn hơn, người tù đã thụ án xong và được thả — mỗi người trong số họ, để có bất kỳ triển vọng nào về một khởi đầu mới, đều phụ thuộc vào một mức độ lãng quên của công chúng. Ta từng gọi điều này là cơ hội thứ hai. Nó dựa, một cách lặng lẽ, vào sự thật rằng hồ sơ là không đầy đủ và trí nhớ thì có thể sai. Tước bỏ sự sai sót đó đi, bạn không có được một xã hội công bằng hơn; bạn có một xã hội trong đó mỗi người bị xích vĩnh viễn vào phiên bản tồi tệ nhất của chính mình từng được ghi lại.

Những người bảo vệ trí nhớ toàn phần sẽ phản đối, không phải vô lý, rằng quên cũng che giấu những sai trái thực sự — rằng kẻ quyền lực xưa nay vẫn dựa vào trí nhớ ngắn ngủi của công chúng để thoát khỏi trách nhiệm. Điều này đúng, và tôi không muốn phủi nó đi. Có một sự căng thẳng thật sự và đau đớn ở đây, và ai giả vờ ngược lại là đang rao bán thứ gì đó. Vấn đề không phải là ta nên quên hết mọi thứ. Vấn đề là quyết định giữ gì và buông gì xưa kia được đưa ra, dù không hoàn hảo, bởi sự bào mòn dịu nhẹ của thời gian và giới hạn của con người. Giờ đây ta đã trao quyết định đó cho những hệ thống mặc định giữ lại mọi thứ, và ta đã làm vậy mà chưa từng thực sự quyết định rằng đây là điều ta mong muốn.

Điều khiến tôi bận tâm nhất là sự đảo ngược âm thầm của gánh nặng chứng minh. Giờ đây quên đòi hỏi nỗ lực chủ động, sự biện minh, thậm chí cả lời xin lỗi — bạn phải yêu cầu xóa, chứng minh quyền được lãng quên, biện hộ trước một nền tảng. Nhớ, ngược lại, là trạng thái mặc định không cản trở. Ta đã sắp đặt các công cụ của mình sao cho thứ dễ dàng nhất, thứ xảy ra khi không ai làm gì cả, là sự lưu giữ toàn phần. Và một trạng thái mặc định, được lặp lại qua hàng tỷ khoảnh khắc nhỏ, không phải là một lựa chọn kỹ thuật trung lập. Nó là một học thuyết về việc một cuộc đời con người là để làm gì — và đó là một học thuyết mà tôi nghĩ chúng ta sẽ bác bỏ, nếu có ai đó nghĩ đến việc hỏi chúng ta.

questions.prompt: What is the 'prevailing assumption' the writer identifies at the start?
options: That digital storage is too expensive to sustain
That perfect recall is straightforwardly an improvement
That people post too many opinions online
That privacy laws have failed to keep pace with technology
questions.explanation: 'The prevailing assumption ... is that this is straightforwardly an improvement. More memory must be better than less.'
questions.hint: The first paragraph names the assumption the essay will challenge.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's central thesis?
options: Forgetting is a defect we should continue to eliminate
Forgetting is a feature we are recklessly dismantling
All digital records should be deleted after a fixed period
Memory and forgetting are equally harmful to society
questions.explanation: 'forgetting is not a defect we have heroically overcome, but a feature we are recklessly dismantling.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer connect forgetting to forgiveness?
options: Forgiveness requires writing down every offence accurately
Forgiveness involves letting a past offence recede and lose its sharpness, which preserved records prevent
Forgiveness is impossible without legal intervention
Forgiveness depends on the offender apologising in writing
questions.explanation: 'To forgive someone is ... to let it recede, soften, lose its sharp edges ... It becomes nearly impossible when the original injury is preserved in high definition.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 3 defines forgiveness in terms of memory softening.

questions.prompt: What does the metaphor 'a prosecutor permanently in residence' describe?
options: A lawyer who lives near the family home
A marriage where preserved transcripts of quarrels are always available to accuse
A spouse who has formal legal training
questions.explanation: 'A marriage in which both parties can produce the exact transcript of every quarrel ... is a marriage with a prosecutor permanently in residence.'

questions.prompt: According to the writer, what does a 'second chance' historically rest on?
options: Legal pardons granted by the state
The fact that the record was incomplete and memory fallible
Public willingness to forgive deliberate wrongdoing
Strict limits on what could be written down
questions.explanation: 'It rested, quietly, on the fact that the record was incomplete and the memory fallible.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 4 explains what a second chance depended on.

questions.prompt: What objection do 'the defenders of total recall' raise?
options: That forgetting wastes valuable storage capacity
That forgetting conceals genuine wrongdoing and helps the powerful escape accountability
That memory is more reliable when it is digital
That forgiveness is overrated in modern society
questions.explanation: 'forgetting also conceals genuine wrongdoing — that the powerful have always relied on the public's short memory to escape accountability.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer respond to that objection?
options: By dismissing it as irrelevant to the main argument
By acknowledging it is true and admitting a real, painful tension exists
By arguing that the powerful never escape accountability
By claiming that wrongdoing should always be forgotten
questions.explanation: 'This is true, and I do not wish to wave it away. There is a real and painful tension here.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 5 shows the writer conceding the point.

questions.prompt: What 'inversion of the burden of proof' troubles the writer most?
options: That courts now require digital evidence in every case
That forgetting now requires active effort while remembering is the frictionless default
That platforms charge users to store their data
That people must prove their identity before deleting accounts
questions.explanation: 'Forgetting now requires active effort, justification, even apology ... Remembering, by contrast, is the frictionless default.'
questions.hint: The final paragraph describes the reversal.

questions.prompt: The phrase 'a marriage with a superior memory' is used:
options: Sincerely, to praise couples who keep careful records
Ironically, to reject the idea that total recall improves a relationship
To describe a real study of married couples
To recommend that spouses record their arguments
questions.explanation: 'is not a marriage with a superior memory. It is a marriage with a prosecutor permanently in residence.' The writer denies the flattering reading and offers the damning one.
questions.hint: Look at how the sentence corrects itself with 'It is ...'.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to the writer's actual stance?
options: We should remember nothing and delete all records
We should not remember everything by default; the decision about what to keep deserves to be made deliberately
Technology companies are the only ones to blame for our memory problems
Total recall is always preferable to fallible human memory
questions.explanation: 'The point is not that we should remember nothing. It is that the decision about what to keep and what to release' was once made by time, and is now made by default-everything systems without our consent.
questions.hint: Paragraph 5 explicitly corrects the extreme reading.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say a 'default, repeated across a billion small moments' actually is?
options: A neutral technical choice with no wider meaning
A theory of what a human life is for
An inevitable result of cheaper storage
A temporary phase that will soon reverse
questions.explanation: 'a default ... is not a neutral technical choice. It is a theory of what a human life is for.'
questions.hint: The closing lines reframe the default.

options: argued or disputed all over again
questions.explanation: 'rather than endlessly relitigated' — having past matters reopened and argued repeatedly, drawn from legal vocabulary.
questions.hint: Think about what 'litigate' means, then add 're-'.

questions.prompt: What does 'anyone who pretends otherwise is selling something' suggest about the tension between forgetting and accountability?
options: That the tension is fake and invented for profit
That the tension is genuine and anyone claiming it is easily resolved is being dishonest
That the writer is uncertain whether the tension exists
questions.explanation: 'There is a real and painful tension here, and anyone who pretends otherwise is selling something' — i.e., denying the difficulty is a dishonest move.
questions.hint: Paragraph 5: what does 'selling something' imply idiomatically?

questions.prompt: Which statement about the essay's structure is accurate?
options: It presents only arguments for forgetting and ignores all objections
It raises a counter-argument, concedes its truth, and then refines rather than abandons its position
It concludes that the powerful should never be held accountable
It argues that human memory is more reliable than digital memory
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 raises the accountability objection, grants it ('This is true'), then narrows the claim to who decides what to keep.
questions.hint: Look at how the essay handles the strongest objection to its case.

questions.prompt: Which idea is NOT claimed by the writer?
options: A society that cannot forget struggles to let people change
All forms of forgetting are good and should never be questioned
Remembering everything has been made the effortless default
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly admits forgetting 'conceals genuine wrongdoing' and calls the tension 'real and painful' — so he does NOT claim all forgetting is good.

cloze.template:
We have built, almost without meaning to, a civilisation that no longer forgets. Every message you have sent for a decade sits {{b1}} on a server somewhere. Your photographs are timestamped, geotagged, and backed up in triplicate. The {{b2}} opinion you posted at twenty-two waits patiently to be quoted back to you at forty. The {{b3}} assumption — rarely examined, almost never defended — is that this is {{b4}} an improvement. More memory must be better than less. Who, after all, would choose to forget?

I want to argue the unfashionable position: that forgetting is not a defect we have heroically overcome, but a feature we are recklessly {{b5}}. The human capacity to let things go is not a bug in the cognitive system. It is part of what allows a life to be lived forward rather than endlessly {{b6}}.

Consider first what forgetting does for forgiveness. To forgive someone is, in practice, to agree to stop {{b7}} a past offence as heavily as you once did — to let it recede, soften, lose its sharp edges. It becomes nearly impossible when the original injury is preserved in high definition, available for instant replay, its every word time-stamped and {{b8}}. A marriage in which both parties can produce the exact transcript of every quarrel is not a marriage with a superior memory. It is a marriage with a {{b9}} permanently in residence.

The same logic scales upward. A society that cannot forget cannot easily allow people to change. The teenager who said something stupid, the convict who served a sentence and was released — each depends, for any prospect of a fresh start, on a degree of public {{b10}}. We used to call this a second chance. It rested, quietly, on the fact that the record was incomplete and the memory {{b11}}. Strip away that and you get a society in which every person is permanently {{b12}} to the worst version of themselves that was ever captured.

The defenders of total recall will object, not unreasonably, that forgetting also conceals genuine wrongdoing — that the powerful have always relied on the public's short memory to escape {{b13}}. This is true, and I do not wish to wave it away. The point is not that we should remember nothing. It is that the decision about what to keep was once made, however imperfectly, by the gentle {{b14}} of time and human limitation.

What troubles me most is the quiet inversion of the burden of proof. Forgetting now requires active effort, justification, even apology. Remembering, by contrast, is the {{b15}} default. A default, repeated across a billion small moments, is not a neutral technical choice. It is a theory of what a human life is for — and it is a theory I think we would reject, if anyone had thought to ask us.

blanks.explanation: 'sits retrievable on a server' — able to be fetched back at any time.
'The half-formed opinion you posted at twenty-two' — not fully developed or thought through.
'The prevailing assumption' — the one currently dominant and widely held.
'this is straightforwardly an improvement' — without complication or qualification.
'a feature we are recklessly dismantling' — taking apart piece by piece.
'rather than endlessly relitigated' — argued over again and again.
'to stop weighting a past offence as heavily' — assigning it weight or importance.
'a marriage with a prosecutor permanently in residence' — the one who brings charges.
'a degree of public amnesia' — collective forgetting; the precise term echoes the medical word for memory loss.
'the memory fallible' — capable of error; the exact word used in the passage.
'permanently chained to the worst version of themselves' — the strongest collocation, evoking imprisonment.
'to escape accountability' — the standard collocation for avoiding being held responsible.
'the gentle attrition of time' — the gradual wearing-away the writer chose deliberately.
'the frictionless default' — happening with no resistance or effort required.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer claims that the default setting of a technology — what happens when no one does anything — is 'a theory of what a human life is for.' Pick one default in a tool you use daily (autosave, read receipts, location history, suggested replies). What theory of human life does that default quietly assume, and would you have chosen it if you had been asked?
